local_names.romanized_local: 荤菜混合炒饭 (hūn cài hùn hé chǎo fàn)

aliases: hūn cài hùn hé chǎo fàn

romanized: hūn cài hùn hé chǎo fàn

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Indian non-veg mixed fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXM covers fried rice as a category. No specific Indian non-veg mixed fried rice code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Indian non-veg mixed fried rice identified in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Non-Veg Mix Fried Rice is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice with Meat and Egg — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Rice, white, fried, with egg and meat (composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for 'Non-Veg Mix Fried Rice' (Indian restaurant style) exists. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for rice-based mixed dishes with meat and egg, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry 21038 (Rice, white, fried, with egg) and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169705 (Rice, white, cooked) adjusted for stir-frying with oil, chicken, prawns, and egg. Protein and fat are elevated relative to plain rice to reflect multi-protein components. Sodium reflects soy sauce and seasoning typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists fried rice (white rice base, stir-fried) with a GI of approximately 55 (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008, Table entry for fried rice). This is used as the closest analogous estimate for Indian-style non-veg mixed fried rice, which shares the same white rice base and stir-frying method. GI = 55, gi_category = 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 47.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and restaurant seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard plate and request reduced soy sauce, as a full serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium — over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 55) and high glycemic load per serving (~47 GL units) due to large white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the full serving delivers a high glycemic load despite a moderate GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, moderate saturated fat, and cholesterol from egg and meat components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and choose smaller portions, as the combination of ~1470 mg sodium, ~4.9 g saturated fat, and ~262 mg cholesterol per serving exceeds recommended single-meal thresholds for cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Non-Veg Mix Fried Rice is an Indian restaurant-style fried rice dish featuring cooked white rice stir-fried in a wok with a combination of non-vegetarian proteins — typically chicken, prawns, and egg — along with mixed vegetables, onion, garlic, soy sauce, and aromatic spices. The dish is prepared using high-heat wok cooking, which imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred flavour known as 'wok hei'. The texture is fluffy yet slightly crispy at the edges, with tender protein pieces and soft vegetables distributed throughout. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a hearty main course. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density (~185 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~8.5 g per 100 g) from the mixed meat and egg components. The dish is relatively high in sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low-moderate boundary), but the glycemic load per standard serving is high (~47 GL units) due to the large white rice base, which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains common allergens including egg, crustaceans (prawns), and soy. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without explicit confirmation from the restaurant.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Indian-style Non-Veg Mix Fried Rice as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed rice-with-meat dish data and USDA SR Legacy fried rice entries, adjusted for the multi-protein composition typical of this dish. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base). Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are composite estimates rather than a direct match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace amounts from cooking oil; this is a conservative estimate. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Dugout - Circular Road) is not confirmed halal-certified; consumers requiring halal food should verify directly. The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI 55 × 85.75 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 47.2. The dish is tagged with 'Butter Chicken' cuisine tag in the source data, which appears to be a platform-level cuisine category label rather than an ingredient descriptor; this tag has been noted but not used as a nutritional signal.
